perturbation.original_text: Recipient's obligations under this Agreement with respect to disclosures of Confidential Information made hereunder shall expire five (5) years from the date this Agreement is executed by the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient's obligations under this Agreement with respect to disclosures of Confidential Information made hereunder shall expire one (1) year from the date this Agreement is executed by the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under applicable state laws, particularly if the information takes longer than a year to become public knowledge or independently discoverable. Many states recognize trade secret protection for longer periods.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by various states, e.g., California)
perturbation.law_explanation: California's version of the UTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year confidentiality period might not be sufficient to safeguard trade secrets, contradicting the intended duration of protection under the law. The definition of trade secret also requires that it derives economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year, creating a potential contradiction with trade secret law. The Law Explanation states that California's version of the UTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage, and that a one-year confidentiality period might not be sufficient to safeguard trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Civil Code section 3426, which is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The Perturbation Explanation states that reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under applicable state laws. Therefore, by shortening the protection period, the changed text creates an inconsistency with state trade secret law, which may require a longer period to adequately protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: At the request of the Discloser, Recipient will promptly return to the Discloser or at Discloser’s instructions destroy any written or electronic Confidential Information and all physical media on which Confidential Information was received from the other Recipient, including any copies thereof, with a letter confirming that the Confidential Information has not been reproduced or copied or that all copies have been returned and that none of the Confidential Information was disclosed to anyone except in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: At the request of the Discloser, Recipient will use commercially reasonable efforts to return to the Discloser or at Discloser’s instructions destroy any written or electronic Confidential Information and all physical media on which Confidential Information was received from the other Recipient, including any copies thereof, with a letter confirming that the Confidential Information has not been reproduced or copied or that all copies have been returned and that none of the Confidential Information was disclosed to anyone except in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly return' to 'commercially reasonable efforts to return' weakens the obligation and could violate data protection regulations in certain states or industries, where strict and immediate return or destruction of confidential data is mandated.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Standards for the Protection of Personal Information (201 CMR 17.00)
perturbation.law_explanation: 201 CMR 17.03(2)(g) requires businesses to take reasonable steps to destroy, erase, or make unreadable personal information no longer necessary for business purposes. Replacing 'promptly return' with 'commercially reasonable efforts' introduces ambiguity and a lower standard of care, potentially conflicting with the regulation's requirement for decisive action regarding unnecessary personal information. Prompt actions show greater adherence to the spirit of the law.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'promptly return' to 'commercially reasonable efforts to return' weakens the obligation. The law snippet 1 (201 CMR 17.00) establishes minimum standards to safeguard personal information. Replacing 'promptly' with 'commercially reasonable efforts' introduces ambiguity, potentially conflicting with the regulation's intent for decisive action regarding unnecessary personal information, increasing the risk of non-compliance. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties expressly agree and acknowledge that any disputes relating to this Agreement will be exclusively adjudicated by federal and state courts located in_______________________.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties expressly agree and acknowledge that any disputes relating to this Agreement will be exclusively adjudicated by a mediator selected by the Agency.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing all disputes to be resolved by a mediator selected solely by one party (the Agency) is likely unenforceable and contradicts the principles of fair dispute resolution and could be seen as a violation of due process or unconscionable under contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA generally favors arbitration agreements, courts can refuse to enforce arbitration clauses that are unconscionable or fundamentally unfair. Having a mediator selected solely by one party is a significant indicator of procedural unconscionability, as it creates a substantial power imbalance and deprives the other party of a neutral decision-maker. This bias contradicts the FAA's goal of providing a fair and efficient dispute resolution process. Furthermore, some states require all parties agree upon a mediator if mediation is required.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. perturbation.justification: The change from 'exclusively adjudicated by federal and state courts' to 'exclusively adjudicated by a mediator selected by the Agency' directly contradicts the principles of fair dispute resolution and introduces a significant power imbalance. While 9 USC 2, as evidenced in both scraped snippets, generally validates arbitration agreements, this validity is contingent upon grounds that exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. The perturbation explanation notes that forcing disputes to be resolved by a mediator selected solely by one party could be seen as a violation of due process or unconscionable under contract law. The modified text removes the ability to have disputes heard in a court of law, and inserts a mediator selected by a single party. The law explanation emphasizes that a mediator selected solely by one party creates a substantial power imbalance and deprives the other party of a neutral decision-maker which is likely unconscionable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of ____________________ without giving effect to any conflicts or choice of laws provisions that would cause the application of the domestic substantive laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of Delaware, without giving effect to any conflicts or choice of laws provisions that would cause the application of the domestic substantive laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware might conflict with franchise laws if the agreement indirectly governs franchise relationships or activities occurring primarily in another state with stricter franchise regulations. Even without explicit franchise terminology, if the arrangement resembles a franchise, those laws might still apply.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Franchise Relations Act protects franchisees operating in California. If the underlying business arrangement, regardless of how it's labeled, meets the legal definition of a franchise under California law (e.g., grant of right to operate a business, marketing plan prescribed by franchisor, payment of a franchise fee), then California law will apply, irrespective of a contractual clause selecting Delaware law. Therefore, choosing Delaware law directly contradicts California's statutory provisions designed to protect franchisees within its borders. This is particularly relevant because the Agency is in the advertising/commercial production business, which could, in some instances, involve franchisee/franchisor-like relationships.

perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from a generic 'State of _____' to 'Delaware' introduces a potential contradiction with the California Franchise Relations Act, as explained in the 'Law Explanation'. If the agreement's practical effect is to establish a franchise relationship within California, as suggested in the 'Perturbation Explanation', then California law would apply regardless of the chosen governing law. The scraped snippets, particularly snippet 1 referencing 'California Franchise Relations Act' within the Business and Professions Code, support the argument that California has specific laws governing franchise relationships, which the choice of Delaware law could undermine, creating a legal contradiction.
